steps.title: Identify the decisive threshold
steps.content: The passage states that a meta-analysis found 176 individuals is the minimum sample size needed to confidently assess population-level handedness in primates. This number is the benchmark against which the 1993 study's claim must be judged. The question asks why the 1993 study's claim about chimpanzee right-handedness should be viewed skeptically.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice and confirm the answer
steps.content:
• Choice A — the sample size on which the claim is based is far below the threshold identified in the meta-analysis.: The 1993 study's sample falls well short of 176—directly explaining the skepticism the passage signals. ✓
  • Choice B — the apparent difference between the two studies' results may be partly attributable to the 1963 study using a different standard to determine handedness than the 1993 study did.: The passage never discusses differing measurement standards between the studies. ✗
  • Choice C — the study that did not find right-handedness in chimpanzees was also based on an insufficient population size.: This refers to Goodall's 1963 study, not the 1993 study whose claim is being questioned. ✗
  • Choice D — right-handedness does not occur frequently enough among gorillas to reliably appear in a sample of only 2 individuals.: The passage is about chimpanzees, not gorillas, and the 2-individual figure is not tied to the 1993 claim. ✗
That matches Choice A — the sample size on which the claim is based is far below the threshold identified in the meta-analysis..
